summary: Fournier identifies a common management anti-pattern: asking questions while simultaneously revealing your preferred answer, which shuts down genuine discussion. She recognizes this habit in herself while commenting on a strategy document and reflects on why it's destructive. The core problem is that stating preferences upfront makes people unlikely to share dissenting views, especially with senior leaders. She advocates for 'getting curious' as a practice—making deliberate space for information before jumping to conclusions. Good decision-making requires understanding nuances from all angles, which is impossible when people fear contradicting you.
key_points: Asking a question while stating your preferred answer cuts off genuine discussion and discourages honest responses
The more senior you become, the harder it is for people to feel comfortable disagreeing with you openly—this is rational self-preservation, not weakness
Highly opinionated engineers must practice making space for information rather than jumping to conclusions to grow as leaders
One of the less-obvious ways we intimidate people is by offering opinions too early, before gathering all the information
Written communication allows you to catch and edit these habits; verbal conversation requires more deliberate practice
money_quotes: I pretend to ask a question but then make it clear that only one answer can be right.
By stating my preference up-front I cut off discussion.
The more senior you become, the harder it is for people to feel comfortable disagreeing with you openly. That is not a sign of weakness on their part!
Most people with any sense of self-preservation know that saying the wrong thing to the wrong person can have negative consequences.

Lucky for you, as the manager, people are going to listen to what you have to say. Unlucky for you, as the manager, if you don't make space for them to say things that you disagree with, you are unlikely to hear important details.
One of the less-obvious ways we make people afraid is by offering our opinions too early, without taking the time to get the rest of the information.
Get curious. You already know there's something you don't totally understand, so hold on stating your opinions until you're sure you've gotten as much information as possible.
key_insight: Leaders who offer their opinions before fully listening create an environment where people withhold important information out of self-preservation, leading to worse decisions.
